J1: Connect to 20x4 LCD display (HD44780) with I2C adapter (PCF85741)
J2: Dual trigger output (digital)
J3: Analog output for current stimulator (DS5)

JP1	ON: Low-pass filter DAC output (might affect slew rate of output)
	OFF: No filtering of DAC output
JP2	1-2: Negative bias on the output amplifier for bipolar output
	2-3: No negative bias for unipolar output
JP3	ON: Set (noninverting) output amplifier gain to 1
	OFF: Set output amplifier gain by RV1 (set RV1=R6 for gain=2)
JP4	ON: Low-pass filter analog output (recommended)
	OFF: No filtering of analog output

RV1: Fine tune to (about) 10k (RV1=R6) for an amplifier gain of 2

Bipolar output settings: JP2 = 1-2 and JP3 = OFF
Unipolar output settings: JP2 = 2-3 and JP3 = ON

Requires: https://github.com/Alarm-Siren/arduino-kicad-library
University of Vienna, Vienna, Austria.

C1=3.3uF

480s=0.693 R2 C1

   = 209M Ohm
GND

GND
C1=3.3uF
1s = 0.693 R1 C1

R1 = 1s/ (0.693 * 3.3uF)
   = 437kOhm
Used this Stackoverflow
t_h = 0.693 * (R_1 + R_2)C
t_l = 0.693 * (R2)C

Want the time separated to be about the same so make R_1 << R_2

say want 1000Hz and 3 uF so (1/1000) =  0.693 * 0.000003 * R_2

R_3 = 481 Ohm

how about .1uF

R_4 = 1 / (f * 0.693 * C) = 1 / (1000 * 0.693 * 0.0000001)
    = 14 kOhm

So say R_4 = 10 kOhm and R_2 = 220Ohm and C = 0.1uF it may be a good start
Pcharge=CV^2
       = (3.3uF)(9V^2)
       = 29.7uJ

So that is about 29.7mJ per 8 minutes charging and discharging 
capacitors. 

If that was all the energy used a CR2032 could run it for a year.

The PCA9554 is identical to the PCA9534 except for the addition of the internal 
I/O pull-up resistors, which prevents the ports from floating when configured as inputs.
Pull-ups are still used in this design so that the '34 can be used if needed.
The PCA9554 is is similar to the PCA9557 except the '54 has an INT pin 
whereas the '57 has a RST pin. Pinouts are also different.

Ground
Liaison :
	- PRSNT1# et PRSNT2# par le GNDD (équivalent présence carte PCIx1)

Remplacement :
	- SMBus -> I2C
	- JTAG -> SPI dont SC1# et SC2#
	- WAKE# -> CS3#
	- CLKREQ -> CLKO
	- RefClk différentielle PCI -> OSC1 et OSC2 pour external clock MCU
	- HSOp(0) soit TX0 PCI -> CAN_1_H
	- HSOn(0) soit TX0 PCI -> CAN_1_L
	- HSIp(0) soit RXO PCI -> CAN_0_H
	- HSIn(0) soit RXO PCI -> CAN_0_L

PS : 
L'utilisation d'un RS485 4 fils est possibles en cablant :
	- HSOp(0) soit TX0 PCI -> DI_A master / RO_A slave
	- HSOn(0) soit TX0 PCI -> DI_B master / RO_B slave
	- HSIp(0) soit RXO PCI -> RO_A master / DI_A slave
	- HSIn(0) soit RXO PCI -> RO_B master / DI_B slave

L'utilisation d'un RS485 2 fils est possibles en cablant :
	- HSOp(0) soit TX0 PCI -> A master / A slave
	- HSOn(0) soit TX0 PCI -> B master / B slave
	- HSIp(0) soit RXO PCI -> NC. ou CAN_0_H
	- HSIn(0) soit RXO PCI -> NC. ou CAN_0_L

ID_SD and ID_SC PINS:
These pins are reserved for HAT ID EEPROM.

At boot time this I2C interface will be
interrogated to look for an EEPROM
that identifes the attached board and
allows automagic setup of the GPIOs
(and optionally, Linux drivers).

Bridging JP1 enables write protection.

DO NOT USE these pins for anything other
than attaching an I2C ID EEPROM. Leave
unconnected if ID EEPROM not required.

- Switching maximum 3 A at minimum 400 Hz PWM
- Qbulk = 3 A * (1 / 400 s) = 0.0075 C
- Cbulk = Qbulk / 12 V = 625 uF
Pull-ups can be omitted assuming the host already has them

Optional shield left floating, since 
it's grounded by the host.
Should be placed as close as possible to 
the connector and the data lines.

The nRF52-Dongle uses decoupling capacitors,
but the datasheet says no external capacitors 
are needed, so leaving them out.

Avoid connecting ground to a common ground point 
(ESD return should be separate from signal return). 
Reset pin can technically be configured to either reset on 
connect or disconnect, but it's pulled up on nRF52-DK and 
Dongle, so pulling it up here as well.

schematic for the QFAA layout. The only exceptions are the 
LEDs, programming pins and reset button.

producing the boards. Should probably revisit this at some point.

You're supposed to drive the 5V line
before outputting video (it's part of
a nominal startup state machine in
the DVI spec, not just for supplying
the DDC + EDID). However most
monitors don't seem to care, and the
VBUS pin is at the far end of Pico, so
this board just hooks up the grounds
and the TMDS lanes, not the +5V. If
you have trouble you can fit this header
and supply 5V to the Sink through it.
GND
Luke Wren

Additional pin functions:
https://pinout.xyz/
JP1
ID_SD and ID_SC PINS:
These pins are reserved for HAT ID EEPROM.

At boot time this I2C interface will be
interrogated to look for an EEPROM
that identifes the attached board and
allows automagic setup of the GPIOs
(and optionally, Linux drivers).

Bridging JP1 enables write protection.

DO NOT USE these pins for anything other
than attaching an I2C ID EEPROM. Leave
unconnected if ID EEPROM not required.

The ESP32 has 10 internal capacitive touch sensors. These can sense 
variations in anything that holds an electrical charge, like the 
human skin. So they can detect variations induced when touching the 
GPIOs with a finger. These pins can be easily integrated into capacitive
pads and replace mechanical buttons. The capacitive touch pins can a
lso be used to wake up the ESP32 from deep sleep.

Those internal touch sensors are connected to these GPIOs:

    T0 (GPIO 4)
    T1 (GPIO 0)
    T2 (GPIO 2)
    T3 (GPIO 15)
    T4 (GPIO 13)
    T5 (GPIO 12)
    T6 (GPIO 14)
    T7 (GPIO 27)
    T8 (GPIO 33)
    T9 (GPIO 32)

RTC GPIOs

There is RTC GPIO support on the ESP32. The GPIOs routed to the RTC
low-power subsystem can be used when the ESP32 is in deep sleep. 
These RTC GPIOs can be used to wake up the ESP32 from deep sleep 
when the Ultra Low Power (ULP) co-processor is running. The 
following GPIOs can be used as an external wake up source.

    RTC_GPIO0 (GPIO36)
    RTC_GPIO3 (GPIO39)
    RTC_GPIO4 (GPIO34)
    RTC_GPIO5 (GPIO35)
    RTC_GPIO6 (GPIO25)
    RTC_GPIO7 (GPIO26)
    RTC_GPIO8 (GPIO33)
    RTC_GPIO9 (GPIO32)
    RTC_GPIO10 (GPIO4)
    RTC_GPIO11 (GPIO0)
    RTC_GPIO12 (GPIO2)
    RTC_GPIO13 (GPIO15)
    RTC_GPIO14 (GPIO13)
    RTC_GPIO15 (GPIO12)
    RTC_GPIO16 (GPIO14)
    RTC_GPIO17 (GPIO27)

The ESP32 has 18 x 12 bits ADC input channels (while the ESP8266 
only has 1x 10 bits ADC). These are the GPIOs that can be used as 
ADC and respective channels:

    ADC1_CH0 (GPIO 36)
    ADC1_CH1 (GPIO 37)
    ADC1_CH2 (GPIO 38)
    ADC1_CH3 (GPIO 39)
    ADC1_CH4 (GPIO 32)
    ADC1_CH5 (GPIO 33)
    ADC1_CH6 (GPIO 34)
    ADC1_CH7 (GPIO 35)

    ADC2_CH0 (GPIO 4)
    ADC2_CH1 (GPIO 0)
    ADC2_CH2 (GPIO 2)
    ADC2_CH3 (GPIO 15)
    ADC2_CH4 (GPIO 13)
    ADC2_CH5 (GPIO 12)
    ADC2_CH6 (GPIO 14)
    ADC2_CH7 (GPIO 27)
    ADC2_CH8 (GPIO 25)
    ADC2_CH9 (GPIO 26)
SPI

By default, the pin mapping for SPI is:
SPI   	MOSI	MISO	CLK 	CS
VSPI	GPIO23	GPIO19	GPIO18	GPIO5
HSPI	GPIO13	GPIO12	GPIO14	GPIO15
I2C

The ESP32 has two I2C channels and any pin can be set as SDA or SCL.
When using the ESP32 with the Arduino IDE, the default I2C pins are:

    GPIO 21 (SDA)
    GPIO 22 (SCL)
Digital to Analog Converter (DAC)

There are 2 x 8 bits DAC channels on the ESP32 to convert digital 
signals into analog voltage signal outputs. These are the DAC 
channels:

    DAC1 (GPIO25)
    DAC2 (GPIO26)

Triple input OR gate. When A14, A15 and MREQ are all low the output is low, otherwise high. A
low output means a ROM memory request is happening. An inverter would make more sense, so a
high output would indicate a ROM access, but having the output active low means that output
can be used more easily for the /CE on the data bus level shifter.

The ROM access signal is then ORed with the /RD signal. If the result of that is low then the
ROM access is actually a ROM read and that can be used to turn on the dbus level shifter.

Pico receives the ROM access signal. This tells it that the Spectrum is either reading or writing the
ROM. It treats both the same, so a write to ROM will load a value onto the Pico's data bus pins. It
doesn't matter, the level shifter which makes the data bus pins visible to the ZX is only enabled on
reads, so ultimately ROM writes don't do anything.
Pico has an output to pull the Z80 /RESET line low, and an input from the user push button.
One GPIO remains unused.

Level shifter for the data bus. This always points from the Pico to the
Spectrum (DIR is low, so B->A). Shifter is always \"off\" unless a read
has happened and the Pico wants to supply data. That way the Pico never
holds data on the bus. /OE is driven by the read-signal detection logic.
When the ROM is being read this IC is enabled; as soon as ZXMREQ goes
high this IC is disabled.

The original circuit gives no sound at 'audio_low',
instead of a -12 dB attenuated sound to have a balanced
mix of digitized sound and sound from YM2149.

Peter Putnik did some research on that problem and proposed 
the following fix :

- Cut between R531 and R539/R530, to insert a 10K resistor
  
- Connect the output of R531 to 'audio_low'. 

See http://atari.8bitchip.info/STEamFix.html for 
the details and how to select this mix in software.

This fix is confirmed by Exxos, see the second section of
https://www.exxoshost.co.uk/atari/last/STEDACFIX/index.htm

PCIe EMS R4.0 V1.0, §1.5: 
- Reference clock (REFCLK-/REFCLK+), must be supplied by the system (see Section 2.1.1)
- Add-in Card presence detect pins (PRSNT1# and PRSNT2#), required
- PERST#, required
- CLKREQ#, optional
- JTAG, optional
- SMBus, optional
- Wake (WAKE#), required only if the device/system supports wakeup and/or the Optimized Buffer Flush/Fill (OBFF) mechanism
- Power Brake (PWRBRK#), optional
- +3.3 Vaux, optional

See also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PCI_Express

Max current output 1000mA
Dropout voltage 1.3V max
3.3V + 1.3V = 4.6V minimum
From PDB or VBUS
VIN: Battery in from PDB 5V+-10%
VBUS: USB in 5V+-5%

TPS2113APW

This configuration prefer VBUS for power input.

When V_SNS is greater than 0.8V, the IC will prefer IN1 to IN2. This means that when VBUS is
active, the device will draw from IN1. When VBUS is Hi-Z (USB disconnected) VSNS will be
pulled low and we will switch to IN2. 

STAT is the output, which is Hi-Z when IN2 is preferred (pulled to 3v3) and low otherwise. 
Therefore, we use stat to measure which source is being used.

A current limiter on ILIM of 1K means we limit current on OUT to 500/1K == 500 mA 
to match USB specification.

See SBVS045C Fig 14.

for the inputs is not the same as the ground for the 
comparators. This means that if V- was referenced to ground,
then the inputs could go below V-, which would not work.

Buffers U9 and U12 are meant for output to an external
device like an oscilloscope. Hence, they are referenced
to the output ground (coaxial connector's ground), rather
than the board's ground, to make sure that the relative
voltage remains constant since the board and output may not
be at the same ground potential.

Some issues that need to be fixed:

1) Grounding / Filtering: It might be necessary to have the signal ground 
and power ground be separate to avoid noise. It may be the case that the power
ground and signal ground coming from the APDs may be at a different voltage.
This could have implications on the layout (the board might need to be 4 layers. 

2) Power source: Should I require a power source like a DC power supply
or try and use the wall plug solution?

3) Power Distribution: It kind of sucks that I need 5 different voltage
regulators. It is doable, but maybe find an alternative.

Provide a few watts of load to the 5V rail, and maybe the 12V rail
if it's using a single component to regulate both rails.

Welcome to my blog: I tried to read the ATX spec to see if
this was still required. The spec seems to indicate it is not,
however I have no idea what ATX supply this will be used with,
and if it's using a linear regulator, this will be useful.

but also maybe not since the VT100 will load the 5V and
12V rails anyway. I have power resistors around, so nbd,
and they're not terribly expensive. You could always make
a bouquet of 20 1/4 watt resistors if you're too lazy
to buy power resistors

IPROPI is 450µA/A.
With 6A peak output this is 2.7mA.
With R=1k we get 2.7V for the 6A.

On VREF, we have 3.3V divided by 560 ohms and 2k7,
this results in 2.73V.

This means, the current limitation will
jump in when the output current is above 6A.
The bulk capacitor is especially
important when switching high
currents, see DRV8874 data
sheet.

In the PCB manufactured at EPFL, one of
the vias (connecting the VBATT pad to the rest
of the circuit) probably broke, causing a bad
connection which lead to the rest of the board
not being powered. To fix this, an extra jumper
was used to connect the VBATT test pad to the
C1 positive pin. The voltage regulating part of
the PCB works great.

and another trace was broken (seen on
microscope), so many extra jumpers were
added on the PCB. This probably wouldn't have
happened in a higher quality PCB manufacturing
facility, this could be added as a useful note
for manufacturing constaints in the future.

Final testing note, the circuit is now fully
operational, all units work and we get readings
from the wheatstone bridge. One caveat being
all broken vias (4 in total) being replaced by
jumpers/solder bridges. One final thing that
could be done is adding a buck converter
to the battery to step down to 3V, so that the
analog value can be read by the motherboard.

ID_SD and ID_SC PINS:
These pins are reserved for HAT ID EEPROM.

At boot time this I2C interface will be
interrogated to look for an EEPROM
that identifes the attached board and
allows automagic setup of the GPIOs
(and optionally, Linux drivers).

Bridging JP1 enables write protection.

DO NOT USE these pins for anything other
than attaching an I2C ID EEPROM. Leave
unconnected if ID EEPROM not required.

Add a PWR_FLAG in Power pins !!:

 Place the PWR_FLAG symbol on the net itself, preferably near the point 
 where power enters your schematic. This informs KiCad that the net is 
 being driven by an external source, typically a power connector, 
 voltage regulator, or other power supply circuitry.
    
 Important Note: Don't place a PWR_FLAG at every power symbol
 (e.g., 3V3, GND). Ideally, use it close to the actual power input
 point.

The PCA9554 is identical to the PCA9534 except for the addition of the internal 
I/O pull-up resistors, which prevents the ports from floating when configured as inputs.
Pull-ups are still used in this design so that the '34 can be used if needed.
The PCA9554 is is similar to the PCA9557 except the '54 has an INT pin 
whereas the '57 has a RST pin. Pinouts are also different.

3 VDD
All digital sensors are specified up to 
125°C, the analog one goes up to 150°C.

This means we might need to estimate the 
actual temperatures from a sensor with
less thermal coupling.

TEMP4 is optional and needs to be taped 
directly under the heat bed. For daily 
use it won't be necessary but it helps 
understand the thermals of the PCB up to
this sensor's T_{max}, and we can check 
how closely the other sensors follow the
actual temperature curve.

TEMP3 is placed at the beginning of the
heating traces. It probably gives the 
most accurate reading up to its T_{max}.
It is also probably the sensor most 
likely to get damaged.

TEMP1 is analog, and probably has the
worst thermal coupling of all sensors,
but it has higher T_{max} and this in 
combination with the worse thermal 
coupling could help reading higher temps
if it still follows the actual temp-curve
closely enough. It also has the least
protocol overhead.

LMT84 is also possible as analog sensor
if ADC reference voltage is set to 1.1V,
but this leaves no headroom for Vin 
measurement.

TEMP2 has better thermal coupling than
TEMP1, but it only works up to 125°C.

Ideally, we could rely on only one probe,
preferably TEMP1 (higher temps, easier
to read) or TEMP2 (easier for manu-
facturing). Maybe we need both to
calibrate the sensors onboard, but TEMP3 
and TEMP4 can probably be left out on the
final PCB

There are also areas on the PCB that can 
be bridged with solder to increase thermal
coupling if it is necessary. This would
also cause a connection to GND, which 
might cause problems with EMI and a noisy
GND, so R12 can be replaced with a ferrite
bead if necessary. If electrical coupling
is good for EMI, but thermal coupling is
bad, we can also connect only one small
part of the large copper pour to GND.

/******    Design notes    ******/

- ZERO_CROSS MCU pin must have pullup enabled
- Zero Cross is triggered a few hundred uS before \"true 0\", this is compensated for in firmware

- Any reasonable sized N-MOS can be used (>20A, >50V)

- 3V3 must be isolated from AC Input
- DCDC-converter has a small input voltage range, therefore a linear preregulator is used
- 3V3 circuit can probably be simplified, i.e wide-input 24V to 3.3V DCDC and no pre/post reg.
- Linear post regulator is used to reduce VDDA noise, but might be unneccesary.
